Document Title: Electrochemical Sensor With Dynamic Self-Calibration for Acetaminophen Detection in Water
Authors: B. E. Alvarez-Serna; D. A. Arcos-Santiago; J. A. Uc-Martín; R. G. Ramírez-Chavarría
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México
Publication Title: IEEE Sensors Letters
Abstract: In this letter, we introduce a self-calibrating electrochemical sensor for water acetaminophen (ACT) detection. The sensor is built upon a graphite pencil lead (GPL) electrode modified with a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) to ensure selectivity. Moreover, using a sparse identification scheme, the sensor is equipped with a dynamic calibration algorithm to increase the sensor accuracy in time-dependent measurements. The sensor performance was evaluated under static and dynamic conditions using ACT solutions prepared in tap water as the matrix. As a result, the sensor achieved a detection limit of 9.3 mg/L, proving to be a viable alternative for quantifying emerging concerns in water. Finally, we show how simple but robust sensor models could enhance the performance of online measurements.
Author Keywords: Sensor systems;dynamic calibration;graphite pencil lead (GPL);molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP);polyaniline (PANI);self-calibrating sensor
IEEE Terms: Sensors;Electrodes;Sensor phenomena and characterization;Electric potential;Calibration;Heuristic algorithms;Graphite;Data models;Sparse matrices;Reproducibility of results

Document Title: The Yucatan HF Radar Network: A Multinational Technology Demonstration to Advance Monitoring of Western Boundary Currents
Authors: M. Smith; S. Glenn; T. Miles; A. H. Knap; S. F. Dimarco; R. M. Jiménez; D. S. de Léon; M. G. Reali; E. Escalante
Author Affiliations: Center for Ocean Observing Leadership, Rutgers University, New Brunswick, NJ, USA; Center for Ocean Observing Leadership, Rutgers University, New Brunswick, NJ, USA; Center for Ocean Observing Leadership, Rutgers University, New Brunswick, NJ, USA; Geochemical and Environmental Research Group, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, USA; Geochemical and Environmental Research Group, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, USA; Facultad de Ciencias, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, MX; Instituto de Ciencias del Mar y Limnologia, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, MX; Laboratorio de Oceanografía Física, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, MX; Laboratorio de Oceanografía Física, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, MX
Publication Title: OCEANS 2025 - Great Lakes
Abstract: The Yucatan High Frequency Radar (HFR) Network is a trilateral collaboration between the U.S., Mexico, and Cuba designed to monitor surface currents in the Yucatan Channel, a critical segment of the Atlantic Ocean conveyor belt. Two long-range CODAR SeaSonde systems were installed on Isla Contoy and at UNAM's Puerto Morelos facility to provide sustained, near-real-time observations of the Yucatan Current, which feeds the Loop Current and impacts hurricane intensification and climate variability. A key innovation is the use of Automatic Identification System (AIS) ship-tracking data to continuously calibrate receiver antenna patterns, eliminating the need for traditional on-water calibration. Hourly surface currents are processed, quality-controlled using QARTOD standards, and publicly distributed via an ERDDAP server. A derived velocity transect product along the strait's axis is used to analyze flow variability and validate ocean models, which often misrepresent the spatial structure observed by HFR. During the 2024 Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR), real-time radar products supported autonomous vehicle missions and targeted ship sampling. Expansion to Cuba and additional Mexican sites is underway, positioning the network as a foundation for Caribbeanwide HFR coverage under GOOS and TAC-OOFS to improve ocean forecasting and hurricane prediction.
Funding Information: National Academies of Sciences Gulf Research Program;
Author Keywords: High Frequency Radar (HFR);Yucatan Current;Loop Current;AIS calibration;antenna pattern;surface currents;CODAR SeaSonde;model validation;ocean observing;adaptive sampling;data assimilation;Gulf of Mexico;western boundary current;QARTOD

Document Title: Early Predictive Modeling of Engineering Student Attrition: An Ai-Driven Machine Learning Approach
Authors: M. F. E. Posadas; N. Á. Esquivel; J. I. V. Sánchez
Author Affiliations: Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Division of Physical-Mathematical, Sciences and Engineering, FES Aragón, Universidad Nacional, Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 World Engineering Education Forum - Global Engineering Deans Council (WEEF-GEDC)
Abstract: Academic trajectory tracking is a crucial tool for evaluating student performance and predicting graduation outcomes. In engineering education, early identification of students at risk of not completing their studies can enable timely intervention. This study focuses on developing an Artificial Intelligence (AI) predictive model for engineering students at the Faculty of Higher Studies Aragón (FES Aragón) of the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), utilizing a classification approach based on early-semester performance indicators. The goal of this study is to develop an algorithm that can identify students who, based on their academic trajectory patterns, may be at risk of dropping out. By identifying these students early, targeted interventions such as tutoring, remedial courses, and other support strategies can be implemented to help them stay on track and complete their university studies. The dataset consists of academic records from multiple student cohorts. Grades from the first and second semesters were encoded to represent exam attempts and passing conditions. Several Artificial Intelligence based classification models were tested using MATLAB. Feature importance was evaluated through correlation matrices and Parallel Coordinates Plot analysis. Adjusting training-test splits and modifying classification costs were also assessed to enhance model performance. Preliminary results indicate that when using first- and second-semester grades alone, classification accuracy reaches 72.48 %, with models such as the Ensemble Subspace K-Nearest Neighbors demonstrating an Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC) of 0.7827. For prediction incorporating data up to the third semester, models achieved a highest accuracy of 78.85 %, with the Medium Gaussian Support Vector Machines model demonstrating a high AUC of 0.8438. Current work focuses on optimizing the model through feature selection and fine-tuning classification weights to enhance predictive capabilities. This work explores the potential of AI-driven predictive modeling in identifying students at risk of academic attrition. While classification models achieve reasonable accuracy, further refinements are necessary. Future work will investigate the integration of additional contextual factors, such as socioeconomic variables and engagement metrics, to enhance the model's effectiveness.
Author Keywords: machine learning;predictive modeling;student attrition;student retention;higher education
IEEE Terms: Support vector machines;Accuracy;Target tracking;Trajectory tracking;Machine learning;Predictive models;Data models;Trajectory;Engineering students;Socioeconomics

Document Title: Data-Driven Power System Linear Model Identification via Quadrature-Based Balanced Truncation
Authors: M. R. Arrieta Paternina; J. A. Moreno Corbea; J. M. Ramirez-Arredondo; J. H. Chow; A. Zamora-Mendez
Author Affiliations: Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Centro de Investigacion y de Estudios Avanzados del IPN, Unidad Guadalajara, MX; Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, US; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: This paper introduces the novel formulation of the quadrature-based balanced truncation (QBBT) method to precisely identify large-scale power system linear models. The QBBT interpolates a directional frequency dataset that is extracted from a dynamic system, following a quadrature rule to be applied from weighted data to derive a linear system model based on approximations of Gramians, and providing an alternative approach to the classic balanced truncation (BT) formulation that makes use of the system model. In this investigation, an accurate theoretical framework is presented to achieve the application of QBBT considering power systems as black-box models. The Boyd/Clenshaw-Curtis (BCC) quadrature rule is implemented by using different adjustments to accurately identify linear models of different order. The attained results and their validation with analytical power system models confirm the method's potential to retail oscillatory dynamics. The QBBT's effectiveness is compared with the Loewner-based frequency interpolation (LBFI) approach and the BT method on the Klein-Rogers-Kundur (KRK) benchmark power system and the equivalent of the New England transmission system - New York power system (NETS-NYPS), achieving a small absolute error in the magnitude of the frequency response of the models of the order of 1x10-3 dB in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods.
Author Keywords: balanced truncation;quadrature rules;system identification;directional frequency data-set;power system linear model
IEEE Terms: Power system dynamics;Numerical models;Accuracy;Power system stability;Modeling;Mathematical models;Data models;Analytical models;Interpolation;System identification

Document Title: Automatic Detection of Dynamic Wind Conditions in Mexican California: A Machine Learning-Driven Advancement in Wind Management
Authors: M. Arellano Vázquez; M. Zamora Machado; M. Robles Pérez; O. Jaramillo Salgado; C. Minutti Martinez
Author Affiliations: INFOTEC, MX; Universidad Autonoma de Baja California, MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, MX; INFOTEC, MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: Accurate detection and classification of wind states is crucial for accurately assessing and predicting wind energy production, fire spread behavior, air quality monitoring, and understanding complex meteorological phenomena. The complex nature of wind necessitates advanced data analysis techniques to extract meaningful patterns from meteorological datasets. This study presents a stochastic wind classification and identification analysis based on a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) clustering method applied to a case study in La Rumorosa'', Mexican California. By analyzing four meteorological variables (relative humidity, atmospheric pressure, wind speed, and wind direction) over five years, the method automatically identifies distinct wind conditions that can be defined as climate states, including well-known regional phenomena like Santa Ana winds and local orographic winds. Accurate detection of wind states enables better forecasting of wind energy potential at favorable sites, wildfire risk management through predicted fire behavior and monitoring pollutant/allergen dispersal patterns. The proposed approach offers a reliable, computationally efficient method for detecting wind patterns, extending to different geographical regions impacted by diverse wind phenomena.
Author Keywords: wind energy;gaussian mixture model;machine learning;wind state detection;climate modeling;Renewable energy prediction
IEEE Terms: Wind speed;Wind forecasting;Humidity;Clustering methods;Gaussian mixture model;Wind energy;Data models;Clustering algorithms;Wildfires;Time series analysis

Authors: A. Sanchez-Ocampo; M. R. Arrieta Paternina; J. M. Ramos-Guerrero; G. E. Mejia-Ruiz; J. M. Ramirez-Arredondo; L. Lugnani; F. Munguia-Perez; A. Zamora-Mendez; J. R. Rodriguez-Rodriguez
Author Affiliations: Centre for Research and Advanced Studies, MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), US; Centre for Research and Advanced Studies, MX; University of Campinas, BR; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: This paper introduces the real-time implementation with the actual hardware architecture environment (HAE) of an online estimation method that tracks the equivalent time-varying inertia in power systems. The proposed method enables automated and accurate inertia estimation, exploiting the ARMAX model representation and the Teager-Kaiser energy operator (TKEO) disturbance time detector. The effectiveness and high accuracy of the proposed framework are successfully validated in laboratory conditions with actual synchronised measurements from Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) over a real-time emulated New England 39-bus system. The estimate is achieved with a relative error ranging from 0.1% to 7%, even under noisy conditions and atypical measurement values. The literature reviewed does not report any estimation method that is more accurate than the one proposed in this work.
IEEE Terms: Estimation;Mathematical models;Phasor measurement units;Real-time systems;Power system stability;Frequency measurement;Accuracy;Time measurement;Noise measurement;Power system dynamics

Document Title: Ownership Authentication and Clinical Data Backup in DICOM Images via Robust Watermarking and Binary Data Hiding
Authors: A. A. Lopez-Martinez; E. Fragoso-Navarro; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; F. Garcia-Ugalde
Author Affiliations: SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacan, Instituto Politecnico Nacional, CDMX, Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Frontiers of Signal Processing (ICFSP)
Abstract: Medical information is very important and delicate due to its relevance in clinical diagnosis, treatments, research and other commercial and non-commercial applications of governmental and private organizations. Recent advances in information and communication technologies have transformed the paradigm of medical data management, but also introduced new risks related to unauthorized access, manipulation, and distribution. To solve part of these problems, this paper proposes a method that employs two information security levels based on robust watermarking and binary data hiding, respectively. Firstly, clinical data is embedded into a logo using binary data hiding. Then, this logo is inserted into the spatial domain of a medical image through block segmentation, statistical information, and convolutional encoding for ownership authentication. Experimental results demonstrate logo robustness against JPEG DICOM compression in conjunction with aggressive attacks like cropping and copy-move, using bit error rate as a metric. The imperceptibility has been measured via peak signal-to-noise ratio and structural similarity index metrics.
Author Keywords: Robust watermarking;Binary data hiding;DICOM imaging;Information security;Ownership authentication;Convolutional encoding
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Image coding;Bit error rate;Authentication;Transform coding;Information security;Watermarking;Robustness;DICOM;Medical diagnostic imaging

Document Title: Step-test based procedure for Super-Twisting Algorithm gains adjustment
Authors: T. N. Medvedeva; O. Y. Sumenkov; L. M. Fridman
Author Affiliations: Mathematical Robotics Science Division, Sirius University of Science and Technology, Sochi, Russia; Mathematical Robotics Science Division, Sirius University of Science and Technology, Sochi, Russia; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 17th International Workshop on Variable Structure Systems (VSS)
Abstract: Super-Twisting Algorithms (STA) is a non-linear PI-like continuous Sliding Mode (SM) algorithm designed to substitute discontinuous control to continuous and to realise a second-order SM for systems with relative degree one. It is able to compensate the Lipschitz disturbances theoretically exactly and ensures finite-time convergence. However, due to the finite-time convergence of STA and the presence of non-idealities in the system, chattering occurs. This paper proposes a step-test based procedure for adjusting the STA gains to minimize chattering amplitude. We assume that system non-idealities can be modeled as a specific first-order system, namely, with time delay, time constant and system gain. The procedure consists of two steps: on the first stage, identifying the parameters of system; on the second step, predicting the amplitude and frequency of the chattering based on the Describing Function approach and the Harmonic Balance equation, then obtaining expressions of the chattering amplitude and frequency as functions of STA gains and, finally, adjusting the STA parameters based on predicted chattering behavior. This approach is verified on both simulations and experiments on a Maxon RE50 DC Motor.
IEEE Terms: Variable structure systems;Delay effects;Conferences;Prediction algorithms;DC motors;Harmonic analysis;Mathematical models;Oscillators;Convergence

Authors: M. A. Chacón; B. C. Sodi; R. D. Serrano
Author Affiliations: Dirección Nacional de Ciencias Espaciales, Universidad Tecnológica de Panamá, Panamá, Panamá; Instituto de Radioastronomía y Astrofísica, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Michoacán, México; Dirección Nacional de Ciencias Espaciales, Universidad Tecnológica de Panamá, Panamá, Panamá
Publication Title: 2024 9th International Engineering, Sciences and Technology Conference (IESTEC)
Abstract: In this paper, we analyze the surface photometry of 42 spiral galaxies from the SDSS Data Release 3 in the r band, focusing on bar identification and characteristics in the local universe. Using ISOPHOTE and P2DFFT, we measure bar length and strength through elliptical isophote fitting and two-dimensional Fourier transforms. Surface photometry is crucial for studying galaxy morphology, revealing information about size, shape, and dynamics. Our study identified barred spiral galaxies with a frequency of 33% using Elliptical Isophote Analysis and 23% using Fourier Analysis. By combining both methods, we achieve a more comprehensive assessment, allowing for the precise measurement of bar length and strength. These methods are complementary and particularly effective for galaxies with large bulges and prominent spiral arms. Our ongoing research aims to extend this approach with automated applications to a larger sample of galaxies.

Authors: M. Iglesias-Rios; U. Pérez-Ventura; L. Fridman; H. Mujica-Ortega
Author Affiliations: Department of Control and Robotics, Division of Electrical Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, México CDMX, C.U.; Department of Control and Robotics, Division of Electrical Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, México CDMX, C.U.; Department of Control and Robotics, Division of Electrical Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, México CDMX, C.U.; Department of Control and Robotics, Division of Electrical Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, México CDMX, C.U.
Publication Title: 2024 17th International Workshop on Variable Structure Systems (VSS)
Abstract: This paper considers the heat transfer processes modeled as first-order differential equations with transport delay controlled by the Super-Twisting Algorithm. In such systems there is inevitably chattering, which consist of fast oscillations in some neighborhood of the operational point. The time constant of the exponential response and the delay of the plant are identified via open loop step test. Based on the identified parameters, the amplitude and frequency of fast oscillations are predicted using the harmonic balance method. It allows to select the STA gains minimizing the magnitude of chattering’s main harmonics. The proposal is tested experimentally on a laboratory prototype.

Abstract: In this paper, we conduct an energy consumption analysis of a broadcast access mechanism over IEEE 802.11 networks. By assuming non-saturated conditions, we provide a statistical model of the energy consumed during one cycle period, defined as the duration between two successful broadcast transmissions. Our results show that the broadcast protocol is a good candidate to be included in future amendments of the IEEE 802.11 standard.

Document Title: Equivalence of Linear Complementarity Problems: Theory and Application to Nonsmooth Bifurcations
Authors: F. A. Miranda-Villatoro; F. Castaños; A. Franci
Author Affiliations: Institute National de Recherche en Informatique et Automatique (INRIA), Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique (CNRS), Laboratoire Jean Kuntzmann (LJK), Grenoble INP, University Grenoble-Alpes, Grenoble, France; Departamento de Control Automático, CINVESTAV-IPN, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, University of Liege, Liege, Belgium
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
Abstract: Linear complementarity problems provide a powerful framework to model nonsmooth phenomena in dynamical control systems. Mimicking the general strategy that led to the foundation of bifurcation theory in smooth maps, we introduce a novel notion of equivalence between linear complementarity problems that sets the basis for a theory of bifurcations in a large class of nonsmooth maps, including steady-state bifurcations in linear complementarity systems. Our definition leads to constructive algebraic conditions for identifying and classifying the nonsmooth singularities associated with nonsmooth bifurcations. We thoroughly illustrate our theory on an extended applied example and on the identification and classification of all possible equivalence classes in 2-D linear complementarity problems.

Document Title: Power System Harmonics Identification Powered by an Eigensystem Realization Approach
Authors: M. G. Juarez; F. Reyes-Maldonado; A. Zamora-Mendez; J. Ortiz-Bejar; J. C. Silva-Chavez; M. R. Arrieta Paternina; V. Torres-Garcia
Author Affiliations: Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Michoacan University of Saint Nicholas of Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; Instituto Tecnologico de Morelia, MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: The proliferation of power electronic devices through the massive integration of renewable energy sources on the small and medium scales in power systems has unleashed high harmonic distortions visible in electrical variables such as voltages and currents. Besides the deteriorating power quality, this fact also imposes challenges in quantifying power quality indicators, advocating novel approaches that deal with this issue. In this context, this paper counteracts such challenges by proposing a strategy to effectively measure the individual and total harmonic distortions in modern power grids. The proposal conceives the well-known system identification technique, the eigensystem realization algorithm, as a filter bank to extract the harmonic components from actual measurements and simulated data. Simulation results of two test systems confirm the effectiveness of the proposed method in attaining reliable estimates for the power system transient harmonics, inter-harmonics, and sub-harmonics. Furthermore, actual measurements corresponding to the energizing of a bank of three single-phase transformers connected in grounded Wye-Delta in an isolated lab-scale system are used. The well-known fast Fourier transform (FFT) approach is also applied to validate the proposition, resulting in the proposed ERA-based method is up to 50 times more precise in some cases than FFT.
Author Keywords: eigensystem realization algorithm;system identification;filter bank frequency response;power quality;total harmonic distortion
IEEE Terms: Power harmonic filters;Harmonic analysis;Filter banks;Finite impulse response filters;Monitoring;Fast Fourier transforms;Proposals;Power quality;Interference;Estimation

Author Affiliations: National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; Synchrotron-light for Experimental Science and Applications in the Middle East, Allan, Jordan; Synchrotron-light for Experimental Science and Applications in the Middle East, Allan, Jordan; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey; National Nanotechnology Research Center (UNAM), Institute of Materials Science Nanotechnology, Bilkent University, Ankara, Turkey
Publication Title: 2025 Conference on Lasers and Electro-Optics Europe & European Quantum Electronics Conference (CLEO/Europe-EQEC)
Abstract: The incorporation of silicon oxynitride (SiON) into silicon (Si) allows us to create a unique material in the field of Si photonics with tunable optical properties [1]. Conventional materials like SixNy, SiOxNy, and Si-rich Si3N4 are utilized for their mechanical robustness, thermal stability, chemical resistance, electrical insulation, and optical transparency [2], [3]. This study presents innovative advancements in silicon photonics through the synthesis of silicon oxynitride-doped silicon (SiON-doped Si) on flexible substrates, enabling the development of distributed Bragg gratings (BGs) for anti-counterfeiting applications. Utilizing a modified plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition system, we achieved low-temperature fabrication of SiON-doped Si, which exhibits a refractive index range from 2.1 to 2.5 at the wavelength of 1310 nm and unique photosensitivity properties across various wavelengths. Our research demonstrates that these flexible BGs can be inscribed with femtosecond laser light, allowing for the encoding of micro-information that is observable under specific viewing angles and light conditions due to change in the absorption of the photosensitive SiON-doped Si thin films layers. The structural integrity and optical characteristics of the BGs were thoroughly analyzed using soft X-ray absorption spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and grazing incidence X-ray diffraction, confirming their potential for high-performance applications in lasers and spectrometers. The findings indicate that the incorporation of SiON-doped Si in wearable technologies not only enhances authentication mechanisms but also opens new avenues for versatile optical devices. This work lays the groundwork for future explorations into advanced materials systems in silicon photonics [4], emphasizing the role of SiON-doped Si in enhancing security features in consumer products.
IEEE Terms: Spectroscopy;Absorption;Optical device fabrication;X-ray lasers;Optical variables control;Silicon;Ultrafast optics;Optical refraction;Optical films;Thermal stability

Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Kiel PowerTech
Abstract: This study applies graph theory to regional frequency analysis in power systems. A novel methodology combines centre of inertia (COI) location with community detection, using an improved label propagation algorithm and spectral centrality analysis. The approach evaluates frequency dynamics by analysing COI behaviour at key buses. The framework divides the grid into well-defined clusters with strong internal connectivity and precise separation. Integrating topological data—such as generation locations and transmission line admittances- precisely identifies each cluster's COI. Simulations on the New England 68-bus test system validate the framework's effectiveness and accuracy.

Author Affiliations: IMAM, UNaM, CONICET, FI, Grupo de Investigación y Desarrollo en Electrónica (GIDE) Oberá, Misiones, Argentina; IMAM, UNaM, CONICET, FI, Grupo de Investigación y Desarrollo en Electrónica (GIDE) Oberá, Misiones, Argentina; IMAM, UNaM, CONICET, FI, Grupo de Investigación y Desarrollo en Electrónica (GIDE) Oberá, Misiones, Argentina; IMAM, UNaM, CONICET, FI, Grupo de Investigación y Desarrollo en Electrónica (GIDE) Oberá, Misiones, Argentina; Universidad Nacional de Misiones (UNaM), FI, Grupo de Investigación y Desarrollo en Electrónica (GIDE) Oberá, Misiones, Argentina
Publication Title: 2025 XXI Workshop on Information Procesing and Control (RPIC)
Abstract: In this work, a genetic algorithm, implemented in the Python programming language, is developed to model a DCDC buck converter in discrete-time. The modeling is performed and validated using experimental signals from a 25 W converter, resulting in an accurate model of the converter.

Document Title: Power System Stabilizers’ Design Through The Eigensystem Realization Algorithm
Authors: M. A. Zaragoza-Luquín; K. Gutierrez-Calvillo; R. Dominguez-Navarrete; F. J. Ruiz-Hernandez; C. Pérez-Rojas; M. R. A. Paternina; A. Zamora
Author Affiliations: Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; UNAM, Mexico City; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE PES Generation, Transmission and Distribution Latin America Conference and Industrial Exposition (GTDLA)
Abstract: This paper proposes an alternative way of designing multiple power system stabilizers (PSSs) for damping electromechanical modes. The approach is based on a system identification method that allows the computation of the first-order sensitivities of the eigenvalues of power system linear models. Thus, the eigensystem realization algorithm (ERA) is used to identify the power system linear model. This proposal derives a state-space representation using power system measurements to compute the power system eigenvalues and their residues for designing a single PSS for damping one mode or coordinated multiple PSSs for damping several modes. The attained results in two benchmark systems, Kundur and the New England transmission system-New York power system (NETS-NYPS), demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed strategy in improving the dynamic response and power system stability for damping one or several electromechanical modes in large power systems, reaching stable behaviors in less than 2s and 7s for the Kundur and NETS-NYPS grids, respectively.
Author Keywords: Damping control;Power system stabilizers;Eigensystem realization algorithm;Eigenvalue sensitivities;Coordinated design
IEEE Terms: Damping;Dynamic response;Power system measurements;Sensitivity;Computational modeling;Heuristic algorithms;Power system stability;Eigenvalues and eigenfunctions;System identification;Proposals

Document Title: Detection of Optimal Echocardiographic Video Segments Using Deep Learning
Authors: I. Perez-Ruiz; J. Perez-Gonzalez; A. Carrera-Pinzón; R. E.; R. H.; B. Y.; A. C. F.
Author Affiliations: Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Universidad Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Hospital Universitario Nacional de Colombia; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas-UNAM
Publication Title: 2025 21st International Symposium on Biomedical Image Processing and Analysis (SIPAIM)
Abstract: The identification of optimal echocardiographic video segments is a critical initial step for reliable cardiac function assessment. This study focuses on the development and evaluation of deep learning models for the automated classification of correct versus incorrect echocardiographic planes in a video sequence, specifically for Subcostal Four-Chamber (S4C) views. A dataset comprising 10 S4C studies was used, from which frames were extracted and preprocessed. Preprocessing included metadata removal, resizing to 224×224 pixels, and data augmentation. The resulting images were divided into training (2068 images per class), validation (517 images per class), and test (200 images per class) sets. Two convolutional neural network architectures, VGG-16 and Vision Transformer (ViT), were trained and evaluated. The VGG-16 model, trained for 30 epochs, achieved a test accuracy of 0.98 and an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 1.00. The Vision Transformer, trained for 30 epochs, yielded a test accuracy of 0.89 and an AUC of 0.97. These preliminary results demonstrate the potential of deep learning models, particularly VGG-16, to accurately identify optimal frames in echocardiographic video sequences.
IEEE Terms: Deep learning;Training;Computer vision;Accuracy;Video sequences;Metadata;Transformers;Data augmentation;Reliability;Videos

Document Title: Optimization of the Safety Margins of the Major Insulation on Oil-Immersed Transformers
Authors: L. M. Espino-Rojas; R. Ocón-Valdez; F. P. Espino-Cortés; M. C. Ortiz-Villanueva
Author Affiliations: SEPI-ESIME Zac., COFAA Instituto Politécnico Nacional, México City, México; Electrical-Electronic Engineering, UNAM, FES-Aragón, Edo. de México, México; SEPI-ESIME Zac., COFAA Instituto Politécnico Nacional, México City, México; ESIME Zacatenco, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, México City, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Electrical Insulation Conference (EIC)
Abstract: The performance of the insulation system is directly linked to transformer life. Therefore, achieving the best insulation system is a key objective during the transformer design. Over the years, many dielectric design criteria have been developed, such as the Weidmann® reference curves and safety margin calculation. This work focuses on optimizing computed safety margins of oil gaps related to the probability of inception of partial discharges in transformer oil without increasing the overall dimensions of the active part of the original transformer design. These margins must be adequate to guarantee that the equipment supports test levels and the dielectric stresses to which it will be subjected during its useful life. An optimization methodology based on genetic algorithms (GA) is developed to maximize safety margins in the major insulation arrangements of a power transformer, considering barriers, oil duct dimensions, and dielectric material properties for mineral or natural esters. The results show that the genetic algorithm yielded considerable improvements in the design of the insulation system of a 20 MVA transformer based on its insulation safety margins, calculated considering the applied potential and impulse voltage test voltages.
Author Keywords: Power transformers;genetic optimization;finite element;insulation design;safety margin;natural ester oil
IEEE Terms: Electric potential;Oils;Windings;Voltage;Oil insulation;Genetics;Safety;Optimization;Power transformer insulation;Genetic algorithms

Document Title: Control of Electromechanical Oscillations with Machine Learning
Authors: F. E. Reyes; A. Zamora; M. R. A. Paternina; J. Ortiz-Bejar; A. Sanchez
Author Affiliations: Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; UNAM, Mexico City; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo, Morelia, Mexico; CINVESTAV, Guadalajara, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE PES Generation, Transmission and Distribution Latin America Conference and Industrial Exposition (GTDLA)
Abstract: This paper uses the HACA clustering method to design electromechanical oscillation control using linear quadratic Gaussian (LQG) control applied to generator exciters and also applied to a static vars compensator (SVC) in a power system. The system is identified by the Eigensystem Realization Algorithm (ERA) for clustering and for tuning the LQG control that is added to the exciters or SVC. As a result, a damping of up to 7% is obtained in the inter-area mode of a test system.
Author Keywords: Machine Learning;Clustering;HACA;Inter-area oscillations;Damping oscillations control;Static Var Compensator;LQG
IEEE Terms: Damping;Machine learning algorithms;Design methodology;Static VAr compensators;Clustering algorithms;Machine learning;Generators;Power systems;Oscillators;Tuning

Document Title: Spatio-temporal Data-Driven and Machine Learning based Applications for Transmission Systems
Authors: F. R. Segundo Sevilla; Y. Liu; E. Barocio; P. Korba; A. Zamora; D. Dotta; F. Bellizio; H. Chavez; H. Jóhannsson; J. Cepeda; J. Cremer; J. Zhao; M. Arrieta; M. Paolone; M. Ramirez; P. Papadopoulos; Y. Susuki
Author Affiliations: ZHAW, Switzerland; Tianjin University, China; University of Guadalajara, Mexico; ZHAW, Switzerland; UMICH, Mexico; Unicamp, Brazil; EMPA, Switzerland; USACH, Chile; DTU, Denmark; CENACE, Ecuador; TU Delft, Netherlands; University of Connecticut, USA; UNAM, Mexico; EPFL, Switzerland; ZHAW, Switzerland; University of Strathclyde, UK; Kyoto University, Japan
Publication Title: 2024 IEEE Power & Energy Society General Meeting (PESGM)
Abstract: This paper summarizes recent advancements on spatio-temporal data-driven and machine learning methods for static and dynamic security assessment, and their particular use cases. It is a collective effort of different research groups members of the IEEE Working Group on Big Data Analytics for Transmission Systems, to provide transmission system operators (TSOs) with innovative tools and ideas for their potential implementation. The algorithms presented here are classified as non-training and training approaches, namely spatio-temporal and machine learning based, considering as input time series from time domain simulations, and or synchrophasor data from wide-area monitoring systems. The efficacy of these algorithms is then evaluated in different IEEE benchmark models and using real system measurements from different countries.
Author Keywords: data-driven;machine learning;static and dynamic security assessment;transient stability;event detection;preventive control;coherency;modal analysis;parameter identification
IEEE Terms: Training;Machine learning algorithms;Power system dynamics;Machine learning;Power system stability;Benchmark testing;Classification algorithms;Topology;Security;Time-domain analysis
